chapter_title: IC-38 General: Full Syllabus Mock Test 2

questions.text: What is the primary objective of the Integrated Grievance Management System (IGMS)?
options: To track and resolve customer grievances across the insurance industry
To issue licenses to insurance companies
questions.explanation: IGMS is an online platform by IRDAI that provides a centralized system for tracking and monitoring consumer complaints across all insurance companies.

questions.text: What is the 'Free Look Period' available to a policyholder after receiving the policy document?
questions.explanation: The 'Free Look Period' allows a policyholder to review the terms and conditions of a life or health insurance policy within 15 days and cancel it if they are not satisfied.

questions.text: Which of the following is considered an intangible product?
questions.explanation: Insurance is an intangible product because it is a promise of future financial protection rather than a physical object you can touch.

questions.text: An insurance agent represents whom in the insurance contract?
options: The Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority (IRDAI)
questions.explanation: In an insurance agency relationship, the agent acts as a representative of the insurance company (the principal).

questions.text: What is 'Bancassurance'?
options: Banks providing loans to insurance companies
The selling of insurance products through a bank's network
questions.explanation: Bancassurance is a distribution channel where insurance companies use bank branches and staff to sell their products to the bank's customers.

questions.text: The professional who assesses and calculates the premium rates and reserves for an insurance company is called an:
questions.explanation: Actuaries are financial experts who use mathematics and statistics to analyze risks and set the pricing and financial reserves for insurance policies.

questions.text: Which principle does NOT apply to Personal Accident and Life Insurance policies?
questions.explanation: Life and Personal Accident insurance are 'Fixed Benefit' policies; the principle of Indemnity (restoring exactly to the financial state before loss) does not apply because human life cannot be quantified in monetary terms.

questions.text: An agent's license in India is valid for a period of:
questions.explanation: In accordance with IRDAI regulations, an insurance agency license is valid for 3 years, after which it must be renewed.

questions.text: What does KYC stand for in the context of insurance?
questions.explanation: KYC (Know Your Customer) is a mandatory verification process to confirm the identity and address of the policyholder to prevent fraud and money laundering.

questions.text: Which of the following is a valid address proof for KYC?
questions.explanation: Passport is a standard government-issued document that serves as valid proof of both identity and address for KYC purposes.

questions.text: The primary function of an underwriter is to:
options: Evaluate the risk and decide on its acceptance and pricing
questions.explanation: Underwriting is the process of evaluating the risk profile of a proposer to decide whether to accept the insurance and at what premium rate.

questions.text: Information that would influence the decision of a prudent insurer to accept or reject a risk is known as a:
questions.explanation: A material fact is any detail that would affect the judgement of a reasonable underwriter in determining the premium or accepting the risk.

questions.text: When must 'Insurable Interest' exist in Marine Hull Insurance?
options: Only at the time of taking the policy
Only at the time of the loss
At both the time of taking the policy and at the time of loss
questions.explanation: In Marine Cargo and Hull insurance, the insured must have an insurable interest in the property at the time a loss occurs to be eligible for a claim.

questions.text: What is the maximum limit of compensation that can be awarded by the Insurance Ombudsman?
questions.explanation: The Insurance Ombudsman has the authority to award compensation (including legal costs) up to a maximum of Rs. 30 Lakhs.

questions.text: Is there any fee charged to the policyholder for filing a complaint with the Insurance Ombudsman?
questions.explanation: One of the key features of the Insurance Ombudsman system is that it provides a free and fair resolution mechanism for policyholders.

questions.text: The Consumer Protection Act protects the rights of:
questions.explanation: The Consumer Protection Act is designed to protect consumers against unfair trade practices and deficiencies in services provided by businesses, including insurers.

questions.text: Under the Motor Vehicles Act, which insurance is compulsory?
questions.explanation: Third-party liability insurance is mandatory by law for all vehicles to ensure that victims of accidents receive compensation.

questions.text: What does a 'Certificate of Insurance' provide in Motor Insurance?
options: Proof of ownership of the vehicle
Evidence that the vehicle complies with the compulsory insurance requirements of the Motor Vehicles Act
questions.explanation: The Certificate of Insurance is a mandatory document that provides legal proof that the vehicle is insured as required by the Motor Vehicles Act.

questions.text: In Health Insurance, Pre-Existing Diseases (PED) are generally covered:
options: From day one without any conditions
After a specified waiting period, usually 12 to 48 months
Only if an extra premium of 100% is paid
questions.explanation: Health insurers typically apply a waiting period (often 2 to 4 years) before they begin covering medical conditions that the insured had prior to policy inception.

questions.text: What is 'Co-payment' in Health Insurance?
options: The insurer pays the premium for the insured
A fixed percentage of the claim amount that the policyholder must bear
Two insurance companies sharing the risk
Paying the premium in two installments
questions.explanation: Co-payment is a cost-sharing requirement where the insured agrees to pay a predefined percentage of every admissible claim.

questions.text: Which of the following is an example of an 'Add-on' or 'Rider' in motor insurance?
questions.explanation: Zero Depreciation is a popular add-on that ensures the insurer pays the full cost of replacing parts without deducting for age-related wear and tear.

questions.text: If a policyholder does not make any claim during the policy year, what benefit do they receive in the next motor insurance renewal?
questions.explanation: No Claim Bonus (NCB) is a discount on the renewal premium offered to the policyholder for not making any claims during the preceding policy year.

questions.text: In Fire Insurance, 'Loss of Profit' policy is triggered only if:
options: The insured decides to close the business
There is a material damage claim admitted under the standard fire policy
questions.explanation: Consequential loss (Loss of Profit) insurance covers the loss of revenue resulting from a fire, but only if the damage to the property itself is also covered.

questions.text: What does a Marine Cargo policy essentially cover?
options: The lives of the crew members
Goods being transported from one place to another
questions.explanation: Marine cargo insurance specifically covers the goods being carried by a ship, aircraft, or land transport against risks of transit.

questions.text: Which policy is designed specifically to cover loss of cash while it is being carried from a bank to the office?
questions.explanation: Money in Transit insurance provides protection against the theft or loss of cash and securities while they are being moved between locations.

questions.text: Public Liability Insurance is highly relevant for:
options: A retired individual living at home
A manufacturing plant or shopping mall
An IT professional working from home
questions.explanation: Public Liability insurance protects businesses from financial losses arising from legal liability for injury or property damage to members of the public.

questions.text: Micro-insurance policies are primarily designed for:
questions.explanation: Micro-insurance products have low sums insured and affordable premiums, specifically tailored for vulnerable and low-income populations.

questions.text: Under the IRDAI (Protection of Policyholders' Interests) Regulations, a claim must generally be settled within how many days of receipt of the final survey report?
questions.explanation: Insurance companies are required to offer a claim settlement or rejection within 30 days of receiving all necessary documents and the surveyor's final report.

questions.text: To become an insurance agent, an individual must pass an exam conducted by a recognized body like:
questions.explanation: The Insurance Institute of India (III) is the professional body responsible for conducting the certification exams required for insurance agents in India.

questions.text: An insurance contract is based on the principle of 'Uberrimae Fides', which translates to:
questions.explanation: Uberrimae Fides means 'Utmost Good Faith', which is the legal duty of both parties to disclose all material facts honestly.

questions.text: What is the penalty for driving an uninsured motor vehicle on public roads in India?
options: Confiscation of the driver's license only
Cancellation of the vehicle's registration only
questions.explanation: As per the Motor Vehicles Act, driving without at least third-party insurance is a punishable offense leading to heavy fines or jail time.

questions.text: A Personal Accident policy provides compensation for:
options: Death or disability directly caused by accidental, external, and visible means
Hospitalization due to a viral fever
questions.explanation: Personal Accident insurance is a fixed benefit policy that pays out only in the case of accidental injury, disability, or death.

questions.text: Permanent Total Disablement (PTD) in a personal accident policy usually pays:
questions.explanation: In the event of Permanent Total Disablement (like loss of both limbs or both eyes), the insurer typically pays the full 100% of the sum insured.

questions.text: In standard fire insurance, what does 'STFI' stand for?
questions.explanation: STFI covers damages caused by natural perils related to weather and water, such as storms, tempests, floods, and inundations.

questions.text: The premium for a Fire Insurance policy heavily depends on the:
options: Occupancy and construction of the building
questions.explanation: Fire insurance ratings are based on the type of construction (e.g., concrete vs. wood) and the nature of the business conducted on the premises.

questions.text: An unexpired risk reserve is maintained by general insurance companies because:
options: Policies may span across two financial years and the risk extends beyond the accounting year
It is needed to pay employee salaries
questions.explanation: Since insurance premiums are collected upfront for a year, a portion of that premium belongs to the period beyond the current financial accounting date.

questions.text: Which clause in a policy penalizes the insured for under-insuring their property?
questions.explanation: The 'Average Clause' ensures that if the property was insured for less than its actual value, the claim amount is reduced proportionately.

questions.text: What is the consequence of non-disclosure of a material fact by the proposer?
options: The contract becomes voidable at the option of the insurer
The ombudsman automatically takes over the policy
questions.explanation: If the proposer hides a material fact, the insurer has the right to cancel the contract and reject any claims based on breach of trust.

questions.text: Which of these is an exclusion under a standard health insurance policy?
questions.explanation: Injuries caused by suicide attempts or intentional self-harm are never covered by health or personal accident insurance.

questions.text: A 'Valued Policy' is most commonly found in which branch of general insurance?
questions.explanation: In marine insurance, goods are often insured for a fixed 'agreed value' because their market price fluctuates constantly during transit.

questions.text: The time period provided to renew a policy after its expiry date, without losing continuity benefits (like waiting periods), is called the:
questions.explanation: The grace period allows the policyholder to maintain coverage continuity even if they are slightly late in paying the renewal premium.

questions.text: Which form of liability insurance is required by doctors and surgeons?
questions.explanation: Professional Indemnity (Errors and Omissions) insurance covers medical professionals against legal costs arising from allegations of medical negligence.

questions.text: Who sets the claim settlement guidelines and protects policyholder interests in India?
questions.explanation: Protecting policyholder interests is one of the core missions of the Insurance Regulatory and Development Authority of India (IRDAI).

questions.text: In insurance terminology, 'Hazard' refers to:
options: The financial amount of a loss
A condition that increases the probability or severity of a loss
questions.explanation: A hazard is a condition (like slippery floors or faulty wiring) that makes a peril (like a fall or fire) more likely to happen.

questions.text: If a building is worth Rs. 10 Lakhs, and is insured for Rs. 5 Lakhs, it is a case of:
questions.explanation: Under-insurance occurs when the sum insured is lower than the actual replacement value of the property.

questions.text: The person who brings a lawsuit seeking compensation for damages in a liability case is called the:
questions.explanation: In liability insurance, the person who suffers a loss and makes a legal demand for compensation is referred to as the claimant or third party.

questions.text: For agricultural farmers, crop insurance protects against:
options: Loss of crop yield due to natural perils like drought or flood
Fluctuation in market prices of crops
questions.explanation: Crop insurance (like PMFBY) covers farmers against the risk of crop failure due to uncontrollable natural factors.

questions.text: What is the role of an insurance broker as opposed to an agent?
options: A broker only sells life insurance
A broker represents multiple clients and can offer products from various insurance companies
A broker settles claims for the company
A broker regulates the insurance market
questions.explanation: Unlike agents who work for one company, brokers are independent consultants who represent the customer and can compare products from across the market.

questions.text: Any alteration to a standard insurance policy is done by passing an:
questions.explanation: An endorsement is the standard legal document used to record any changes to the existing terms of an insurance policy.

questions.text: If a vehicle is completely destroyed in an accident and cannot be repaired, it is treated as a:
questions.explanation: Constructive Total Loss (CTL) occurs when the cost of repairing the vehicle exceeds 75% of its insured declared value (IDV).
